coredata.dc:title: Enhanced catalytic performance of CQDs/Ba0.9Ca0.1TiO3 through up-conversion luminescence effect and photo-piezoelectric co-catalysis
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier Ltd and Techna Group S.r.l. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd and Techna Group S.r.l. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Yunhui
Zhang, Chenxiao
Zhang, Ting
Wang, Xiaoyang
Yang, Xiaotong
Zeng, Dongmei
Zou, Minmin
Zhang, You
coredata.dc:description: A series of carbon quantum dots/Ba0.9Ca0.1TiO3 (CQDs/BCT) composites with excellent catalytic performance was synthesized by hydrothermal method. The catalytic degradation experiment of model pollutant Rhodamine B (RhB) dye was carried out. The up-conversion luminescence properties of carbon quantum dots and their impact and mechanism on the catalytic performance of Ba0.9Ca0.1TiO3 were studied. Results indicate that CQDs/Ba0.9Ca0.1TiO3 can absorb not only ultraviolet light but also near-infrared and visible light due to the up-conversion luminescence effect of carbon quantum dots, thereby enhancing the light utilization efficiency and improving the photocatalytic degradation efficiency. The degradation rate of RhB by CQDs/BCT was significantly enhanced, about 3.5 times higher than that of BCT after 120 min under simulated sunlight. Furthermore, a pressure field of ultrasonic vibration was also introduced during illumination to produce a photo-piezoelectric co-catalysis due to the piezoelectric effect of Ba0.9Ca0.1TiO3, which is beneficial to the separation of photogenerated carriers and further enhancement of the catalytic performance. Finally, an excellent catalytic performance for CQDs/BCT was obtained, completely degrading RhB within only 80 min under both ultrasound and simulated sunlight. This study can significantly improve the catalytic performance of BCT through the up-conversion luminescence effect of CQDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: C. up-conversion luminescence
C. Photocatalytic property
D. Barium calcium titanate ceramics
A. Hydrothermal method

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Possible mechanism of the photo-piezoelectric co-catalysis for CQDs/BCT

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2025 Elsevier Ltd and Techna Group S.r.l. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

item-info.ce:copyright: Elsevier Ltd and Techna Group S.r.l.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The XRD patterns of BCT and CQDs/BCT.
The SEM images of BCT(a) and 20-CQDs/BCT(b); corresponding EDS results of 20-CQDs/BCT(c-g); the TEM images of 20-CQDs/BCT(h-i).
(a) XPS spectra of 20-CQDs/BCT and BCT, and High-resolution XPS spectra of (b) Ba 3d (c) Ca 2p, (d) Ti 2p, (e) O 1s and (f) C 1s.
The UV–Vis DRS (a) and the tauc plots (b) of BCT and CQDs/BCT.
The up-conversion luminescence spectra of CQDs.

UV–Vis absorption spectra of RhB under simulated solar light for (a) BCT, (b) 10-CQDs/BCT, (c) 20-CQDs/BCT, and (d) 30-CQDs/BCT, (e) the degradation rate curves of BCT and CQDs/BCT, (f) Pseudo-first-order kinetic curves for photocatalytic degradation of BCT and CQDs/BCT.
The degradation of RhB with different scavengers.
(a) the EIS diagrams and (b) transient photocurrent of BCT and 20-CQDs/BCT.

UV–Vis absorption spectra of RhB under ultrasonic vibration for (a) 20-CQDs/BCT and (c) BCT, under ultrasonic vibration and simulated sunlight irradiation for (b) 20-CQDs/BCT and (d) BCT, (e) degradation rate curves of 20-CQDs/BCT and BCT under different conditions.
Mechanism of RhB degradation by CQDs/BCT.
Synthesis Process of the CQDs/BCT Composites.
Performance of some BaTiO3-based photocatalyst under solar light.

head.ce:title: Enhanced catalytic performance of CQDs/Ba0.9Ca0.1TiO3 through up-conversion luminescence effect and photo-piezoelectric co-catalysis

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of New Materials and Chemical Engineering, Beijing Institute of Petrochemical Technology, Beijing, 102617, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of New Materials and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of New Materials and Chemical Engineering, Beijing Institute of Petrochemical Technology, Beijing, 102617, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A series of carbon quantum dots/Ba0.9Ca0.1TiO3 (CQDs/BCT) composites with excellent catalytic performance was synthesized by hydrothermal method. The catalytic degradation experiment of model pollutant Rhodamine B (RhB) dye was carried out. The up-conversion luminescence properties of carbon quantum dots and their impact and mechanism on the catalytic performance of Ba0.9Ca0.1TiO3 were studied. Results indicate that CQDs/Ba0.9Ca0.1TiO3 can absorb not only ultraviolet light but also near-infrared and visible light due to the up-conversion luminescence effect of carbon quantum dots, thereby enhancing the light utilization efficiency and improving the photocatalytic degradation efficiency. The degradation rate of RhB by CQDs/BCT was significantly enhanced, about 3.5 times higher than that of BCT after 120 min under simulated sunlight. Furthermore, a pressure field of ultrasonic vibration was also introduced during illumination to produce a photo-piezoelectric co-catalysis due to the piezoelectric effect of Ba0.9Ca0.1TiO3, which is beneficial to the separation of photogenerated carriers and further enhancement of the catalytic performance. Finally, an excellent catalytic performance for CQDs/BCT was obtained, completely degrading RhB within only 80 min under both ultrasound and simulated sunlight. This study can significantly improve the catalytic performance of BCT through the up-conversion luminescence effect of CQDs.

ce:section.ce:para: Water, as the cornerstone of ecology, is increasingly polluted by the continuous discharge of various wastes, posing serious threats to public health [1]. Therefore, the development of novel, environmentally friendly technologies and materials for sewage treatment is important. Photocatalytic degradation technology is a water remediation method in which photocatalyst exhibits oxidation and reduction ability to remove water pollutants under solar radiation. Due to the advantages of energy saving, high efficiency, renewable and sustainable, photocatalytic degradation technology has applied in the field of water treatment [2].

Some materials, such as TiO2, ZnO, BiVO4 et al., have been used as photocatalysts. BaTiO3 can be also used as a photocatalyst due to its characteristics of excellent optical stability, thermal stability and low cost, which has been attracted more and more attentions [3,4]. Many studies have focused on investigating and enhancing the photocatalytic performance of BaTiO3 through element doping and heterostructure construction. For example, Khan et al. [5] studied photocatalytic performance of Ag-doped BaTiO3 NPs and found that its photocatalytic activity increased by 40 %. Kappadan et al. [6] prepared BaTiO3/ZnO (BTZ) heterojunctions. Compared with BaTiO3, the degradation performance of BTZ heterostructure was increased 45 % within 15 min. What's more, BaTiO₃ is a typical ferroelectric material with notable piezoelectric properties [3] that can achieve photo-piezoelectric co-catalysis by producing built-in electric field to promote the separation of photogenerated carriers and further enhance the catalytic performance. For instance, Yu et al. [7] investigated the photo-piezoelectric co-catalytic performance of Li/La doped BaTiO3 nanosheets, which was much higher than that of single photocatalysis. Therefore, BaTiO3 has been recognized as a promising and effective photocatalyst.

However, BaTiO3, as a photocatalyst, can only absorb ultraviolet light but not the visible or near-infrared light due to its wide band gap of about 3.2 eV. Wu et al. [8] found that Cr-doped BaTiO3 can only absorb ultraviolet light (λ < 400 nm), but exhibit very limited photocatalytic performance under visible light irradiation (400 nm–720 nm). Thus, broaden the light absorption range of BaTiO3 becomes very critical to its application. Researches have shown that the up-conversion luminescence effect can convert visible and near-infrared light into ultraviolet light [9] which can be absorbed by BaTiO3, through an efficient two or multi-photon active process [10]. C quantum dots (CQDs) possess the up-conversion luminescence characteristic [11], which has been introduced into TiO2 to broaden its light absorption range [12]. Guan et al. [13] successfully sensitized TiO2 nanotube array (NTA) films with CQDs and extended the light absorption of TiO2 films into visible region. Similarly, Jin et al. [14] studied the impact of N-doped CQDs on the photocatalytic degradation activity of TiO2 powder, finding that the light absorption range was broadened and the photocatalytic capability was enhanced. Therefore, it is speculated that introducing CQDs could also broaden the light absorption range and increase the light absorption efficiency of BaTiO3. What's more, the element doping can increase the piezoelectric coefficient to improve the piezoelectric properties. Research has demonstrated that the addition of 2 wt%-34 wt% Ca2+ to BaTiO3 can significantly increase the piezoelectric coefficient from 180 pC N−1 to 310 pC N−1 [15] and increase the Curie temperature [16].

In this study, a series of CQDs/Ba0.9Ca0.1TiO3 catalysts is prepared by hydrothermal method. Both the up-conversion luminescence characteristic of CQDs and its influence on the photocatalytic performance of Ba₀.₉Ca₀.₁TiO₃ were investigated. What's more, an external pressure field was also introduced during the photocatalytic process to further increase the photocatalytic properties of Ba₀.₉Ca₀.₁TiO₃ by photo-piezoelectric co-catalysis. Results indicated that the degradation rate of RhB by 20-CQDs/BCT was significantly enhanced, about 3.5 times higher than that of BCT after 120 min under simulated sunlight due to the up-conversion luminescence effect. Furthermore, 20-CQDs/BCT can completely degrade the RhB only after 80 min under both ultrasound and simulated sunlight. This study can significantly improve the catalytic performance of BCT through the up-conversion luminescence effect of CQDs.

Ba0.9Ca0.1TiO3 was prepared using a two-step hydrothermal process. Initially, 1.25 g TiO2 (purity 99.8 %, Innochem, Beijing, China) and 24.24 g NaOH (purity 99 %, Innochem, Beijing, China) were dissolved in 60 mL deionized water, which was stirred for 1 h and then transferred into a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave to heat at 180 °C for 24 h. After filtrating and washing, the obtained white precipitates, Na2Ti3O7, were dispersed into 0.2 M HCl solution (AR, Sinopharm, Shanghai, China) and stirred for 4 h to prepare H2Ti3O7, which was washed by deionized water until the pH reached neutral and dried at 60 °C for 12 h. Subsequently, the compounds Ba(OH)2·8H2O (purity 98 %, Innochem, Beijing, China), Ca(OH)2 (purity 95 %, Innochem, Beijing, China) and H2Ti3O7 were dissolved into 60 mL deionized water at a molar ratio of 27:3:5, which was placed in a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated at 210 °C for 85 min. After filtration, washing and drying at 60 °C for 12 h, the Ba0.9Ca0.1TiO3 was obtained.

CQDs were prepared using a simple hydrothermal process. Initially, 0.13 M ascorbic acid (AR, Sinopharm, Shanghai, China) was dissolved into a mixture of deionized water and absolute ethyl alcohol, which was stirred for 30 min. Then, the solution was transferred into a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 3 h. After the hydrothermal treatment, the upper brown solution was separated by extracting with 50 mL dichloromethane, and then neutralized using 0.1 M NaOH. Finally, the CQDs solution was obtained after dialysis for 24 h.

0.1 g prepared Ba0.9Ca0.1TiO3 and different volumes of CQDs solution (10 mL, 20 mL, 30 mL) were respectively mixed in a beaker and reacted in a water bath at 80 °C for 2 h. A series of CQDs/Ba0.9Ca0.1TiO3 composites were obtained. The process is shown in Scheme 1 . Based on the volume of added CQDs solution, the composites were named respectively as 10-CQDs/BCT, 20-CQDs/BCT and 30-CQDs/BCT.

Crystalline structure of the composites was determined by an X-ray diffraction (XRD) (D8, Advanced Bruker) with Cu Kα source. Morphology of the composites was characterized by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) (Apreo, Thermo Fisher). The light absorption behavior of as-prepared CQDs/BCT samples and the degradation curve of RhB were investigated by Ultraviolet–visible spectrophotometer (UV–Vis) (UV-2600, SHIMADZU). The surface chemical state analysis was performed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (Nexsa G2, Thermo Fisher). The up-conversion luminescence characteristic was confirmed by Fluorescence Spectrophotometer (FLS) (F-7100, Hitachi). The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy and transient photocurrent measurements were executed using an electrochemical workstation (CHI660D, CH Instruments).

The photocatalytic activity of CQDs/BCT was investigated by degradation of RhB under Xenon lamp irradiation. Firstly, 50 mg CQDs/BCT powder was added into 50 mL 10 mg/L RhB solution. The mixture was stirred for 1 h in dark conditions to establish adsorption-desorption equilibrium. Then, under the irradiation of 300 W xenon lamp, 3 mL suspension was centrifuged every 20 min until the reaction lasted for 120 min. The extracted suspension was analyzed using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu UV2600) at 553 nm to determine the residual RhB concentration and investigate its photocatalytic behavior. The degradation efficiency of RhB was calculated using Eq (1), where
C 0
is the concentration before irradiation,
C
is the concentration at the time of sampling time and
η
stands for degradation efficiency [17].
(1)
η =
[
1 −
(
C /
C 0
)
]
× 100 %

The decomposition kinetics of RhB solution was studied according to the simplified Langmur-Hinshelwood model, which allows the reaction rate constant to be used as a metric for comparing photocatalytic performance. The photocatalytic process should follow a pseudo-first-order kinetic reaction model, as shown in Eq (2), where
k
represents the rate constant for the pseudo-first-order kinetic reaction,
t
stands for photocatalytic reaction time [18].
(2)
ln
(
C 0
/ C
)
= k t

Free radical scavenging experiments were conducted to identify the main active substances involved in the degradation of RhB molecules. The impact of 1 mM ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid disodium salt (EDTA-2Na, a hole scavenger), 1 mM isopropanol (IPA, a hydroxyl radical scavenger) and nitrogen gas (N2, a superoxide anion radical scavenger) on the photocatalytic degradation of RhB dye by CQDs/BCT was respectively investigated in an RhB solution.

The photo-piezoelectric co-catalysis activity of CQDs/BCT in the degradation of RhB was studied under both the ultrasonic vibration and Xenon lamp irradiation. The measurement process was similar with that of photocatalytic activity in 2.4. The ultrasonic vibration power is 360 W and its frequency is 40 KHz.

The XRD patterns of BCT and CQDs/BCT composites are presented in Fig. 1 . The diffraction peaks of prepared BCT are consistent with the standard card of BaTiO3 (JCPDS 76–0744), corresponding to a pure perovskite phase with a point group of P4mm. The patterns of CQDs/BCT exhibit similar peaks with BCT, confirming that the incorporation of CQDs does not change the crystal structure of BCT. No diffraction peak of CQDs is observed, which may be attributed to amorphous or low crystallinity of CQDs, similar with that in the previous reports [19,20]. To verify some Ca successfully doping into BTO lattice, the position of (101) peak for BCT was compared to BTO. As shown in the enlarged view in Fig. 1, the (101) peaks of all BCT shift towards higher angles, indicating that some Ca has been successfully doped into the lattice.

The SEM images of BCT and 20-CQDs/BCT are shown in Fig. 2 (a and b). It can be observed that both BCT and 20-CQDs/BCT show particles with an average size of several hundred nanometers, meaning that CQDs does not alter the morphology of BCT. Since the size of CQDs is extremely small, they are not individually observed in SEM image. To confirm the existent of CQDs, energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) was performed. The EDS results of 20-CQDs/BCT inset Fig. 2(b) reveals the presence of five elements—Ba, Ca, Ti, O, and C. Fig. 2(c–g) present the mapped images of these elements, corresponding to the area highlighted Fig. 2(b). All these elements are uniformly distributed throughout the composite, suggesting the successful introducing and uniform distribution of CQDs into BaTiO3, which confirms the synthesis of CQDs/BCT composite. SEM images of other CQDs/BCT compositions are similar with that of 20-CQDs/BCT. To further investigate the loading of CQDs on the BCT surface, TEM analysis was performed (Fig. 2(h and i)). The presence of numerous black spots on the BCT surface indicates the successful incorporation of CQDs. Furthermore, based on the SEM images, the particle size of BCT is estimated to be approximately 100–500 nm. The SEM images also show that the particle size of BCT did not change significantly after incorporating CQDs. This could be attributed to the small particle size of the CQDs, which are only a few nanometers in size as observed in TEM images.

To further identify the elemental species and chemical states of 20-CQDs/BCT, XPS was also carried out. Fig. 3(a) is the typical full survey XPS spectrum of 20-CQDs/BCT, clearly indicating the presence of Ba, Ca, Ti, O, and C. Fig. 3(b–e) shows the high-resolution XPS spectra of different elements in the 20-CQD/BCT and BCT. In the high-resolution XPS spectra of 20-CQD/BCT, the Ba 3d spectrum exhibits peaks at 778.5 eV and 793.8 eV, respectively corresponding to Ba 3d 3/2 and Ba 3d 5/2, confirming a +2 valence state for Ba [21]. The Ca 2p spectrum reveals peaks at 347.5 eV and 351.0 eV, respectively corresponding to Ca 2p 3/2 and Ca 2p 1/2 [22]. The Ti 2p spectrum shows peaks at 457.9 eV and 463.6 eV, indicating the existence of Ti4+ [23]. And the O 1s peak at 528.52 eV is attributed to lattice oxygen, while the peak at 530.92 eV is associated with oxygen adsorbed on the sample surface [24]. Finally, Fig. 3 (f) shows the C 1s spectrum, which can prove the existence of carbon quantum dots, and the peaks at 284.8eV, 285.5eV, and 289.2eV correspond to C=C & C-H [25], C-O, and C=O [26], respectively. Compared with BCT, the XPS peaks of all elements in the 20-CQDs/BCT shift towards higher binding energies. This shift typically indicates a change in the electronic environment of the elements in the sample. Specifically, the electrons of BCT flow to CQDs [27]. The results of XPS spectra identify the loading of carbon quantum dots on BCT, which is consistent with the results of EDS.

The ultraviolet–visible diffuse reflection spectra (UV–Vis DRS) of BCT and CQDs/BCT composites are presented in Fig. 4 (a) . The absorption edge of BCT is observed at only 408 nm. With the content of CQDs increasing, the absorption edge of CQDs/BCT shifted towards the red, reaching a maximum at 435 nm for 20-CQDs/BCT, and then decreased a little for 30-CQDs/BCT, which might be due to more CQDs covering the surface and active sites of Ba0.9Ca0.1TiO3 and affecting the light absorption. The redshift of absorption edge for CQDs/BCT indicates that the incorporation of CQDs can enhance the light absorption of BCT, making it absorb visible light. The Tauc plots for BCT and CQDs/BCT are shown in Fig. 4 (b), and the band gap of CQDs/BCT are calculated using Eq (3) [28].
(3)
(
α h ν
)
n
=
B 0
(
h ν −
E g
)

The bandgap values for BCT, 10-CQDs/BCT, 20-CQDs/BCT, and 30-CQDs/BCT are 3.16 eV, 3.14 eV, 3.06 eV and 3.09 eV, respectively. It can be seen that the band gaps of 10-CQDs/BCT, 20-CQDs/BCT and 30-CQDs/BCT are reduced compared to BCT. Especially, 20-CQDs/BCT has the smallest band gap, which would enable 20-CQDs/BCT to absorb light more easily and produce more photogenerated carriers.

To further clarify the effect of CQDs, Fig. 5 shows the up-conversion luminescence characteristic of CQDs. As the excitation light wavelength ranging from 600 nm to 900 nm, the up-conversion emitted light of CQDs is primarily concentrated on 200 nm–250 nm and 350 nm–450 nm, especially the strongest emission peak at approximately 425 nm when excited by 850 nm near-infrared light. It can be seen in Fig. 4(a) that the absorption edge of 10-CQDs/BCT, 20-CQDs/BCT and 30-CQDs/BCT are respectively about 417 nm, 435 nm, and 425 nm, meaning that the up-conversion emission light from long wavelength light can be easily absorbed by CQDs/BCT to produce more photogenerated carriers and improve the photocatalytic performance. This enables the catalyst to utilize a broader spectrum of light, extending the activation range from UV to visible and near-infrared light. Therefore, the up-conversion luminescence properties of CQDs play a crucial role in improving the photocatalytic activity of BCT.

To confirm the impact of CQDs on the photocatalytic performance of BCT, both BCT and CQDs/BCT composites were respectively dispersed in a RhB dye solution and the degradation performance was investigated under the same illumination time. The UV–Vis absorption spectra of the degradation process for BCT and CQDs/BCT are shown in Fig. 6 (a–d). For all UV–Vis absorption spectra, a decreased absorption peak as illumination time was observed, indicating that both BCT and CQDs/BCT exhibit photocatalytic degradation capability. However, compared to BCT, the absorption peak of CQDs/BCT decreased more significantly after the same illumination time, suggesting that CQDs can indeed enhance the photocatalytic performance of BCT due to its up-conversion luminescence effect. Based on UV–Vis absorption spectra, the degradation rates of CQDs/BCT with different CQDs contents were calculated by Eq (1) and plotted in Fig. 6(e). After illumination for 120 min, the degradation rate of RhB solution for 10-CQDs/BCT, 20-CQDs/BCT and 30-CQDs/BCT respectively reached 62.3 %, 90.3 % and 53.3 %. 20-CQDs/BCT has obtained the highest degradation efficiency and photocatalytic activity. This can be attributed to its broader light absorption edge of 435 nm and more absorbed fluorescence generated by the up-conversion luminescence effect of CQDs. Table 1 lists some BaTiO3-based photocatalysts and their properties under solar light in previous researches. It can be seen that the synthesized CQDs/Ba0.9Ca0.1TiO3 in this research has got the highest degradation rate under the same illumination time, suggesting that the photocatalytic performance of CQDs/Ba0.9Ca0.1TiO3 has been effectively enhanced. The photocatalytic degradation kinetics of the RhB solution was investigated using the simplified Langmuir-Hinshelwood kinetic model. Results in Fig. 6(f) indicate that all photocatalytic degradation processes follow the first-order kinetic model. After the same illumination time, the catalysts exhibited different reaction rate constants of 0.0021 min−1, 0.00853 min−1, 0.01892 min−1 and 0.00669 min−1, respectively. 20-CQDs/BCT has obtained the highest catalytic reaction rate, which is consistent with the results of degradation rates.

To further investigate the mechanism of photocatalytic degradation by CQDs/BCT, RhB was used as a model pollutant, and 20-CQDs/BCT was employed as the photocatalyst to identify the active species potentially involved in the dye degradation process. Prior to the experiment, 1 mM EDTA-2Na (hole quench agent), 1 mM IPA (·OH quench agent) and N2 (·O2 − quench agent) were added to examine their effects on the photocatalytic degradation of RhB by 20-CQDs/BCT [19,33]. The results are shown in Fig. 7 . It is evident that the addition of EDTA-2Na, IPA, and N2 significantly inhibited the degradation of RhB, with degradation rates decreasing about 68.5 %, 69.9 %, and 57.9 %, respectively. These findings indicate that ·OH, ·O2 − and holes are all crucial in the photocatalytic degradation of dyes mediated by CQDs/BCT.

The photoelectrochemical activity of BCT and 20-CQDs/BCT were also investigated by homogeneously mixing into PVDF and NMP and spin-coating onto FTO substrates (1 cm2). Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and transient photocurrent response results were shown in Fig. 8 . The arc radius of the EIS curve for BCT is larger than that for 20-CQDs/BCT, indicating that 20-CQDs/BCT possessed a lower charge-transfer resistance, which is beneficial for the separation and migration of photogenerated electrons and holes [34,35]. This can be attributed to the fact that CQDs conducts electricity well and can promote electron transfer. Fig. 8(b) shows the transient photocurrent response of BCT and 20-CQDs/BCT. It can be seen that the transient photocurrent for 20-CQDs/BCT is notably higher than that for BCT, increasing from 1.47 μA/cm2 to 5.03 μA/cm2. The reason might be that the up-conversion effect of CQDs could convert visible and near-infrared light into shorter wavelengths that can be absorbed by BCT, and enhance the absorption of sunlight to generate more electrons and holes. This result is consistent with the EIS analysis.

To further enhance the catalytic performance of 20-CQDs/BCT, a synergism of photocatalysis and piezoelectric effect, known as photo-piezoelectric co-catalysis, was carried out by introducing ultrasonic vibration during illumination. Fig. 9 ( a, c) is the UV–Vis absorption spectra of RhB degraded by 20-CQDs/BCT and BCT under only ultrasonic vibration. It can be seen that the characteristic peak of 554 nm for RhB significantly decreased, confirming that the piezoelectric effect of 20-CQDs/BCT and BCT could also degrade RhB. Fig. 9 (b, d) is the UV–Vis absorption spectra of RhB degraded by 20-CQDs/BCT and BCT under both ultrasonic vibration and illumination. It is obvious that a higher degree of RhB degradation was achieved by photo-piezoelectric co-catalysis. To compare the activity of photocatalysis, piezoelectric catalysis and photo-piezoelectric co-catalysis, the degradation rates of RhB under different conditions were plotted in Fig. 9 (e). The piezoelectric catalytic efficiency of BCT reached 73 %. However, this efficiency is lower than that of the 20-CQDs/BCT composite, which can be attributed to the enhanced charge transfer on the BCT surface facilitated by the presence of CQDs [36]. Similarly, due to the up-conversion luminescence effect of CQDs, the photo-piezoelectric co-catalytic efficiency of BCT was 94 %, which is also lower than that of 20-CQDs/BCT. Especially, for 20-CQDs/BCT, after catalytic reaction of 120 min, the degradation rate of piezoelectric catalysis and photocatalysis reached respectively 80.5 % and 90.3 %. While, for photo-piezoelectric co-catalysis, completely degraded RhB within 80min. The photo-piezoelectric co-catalysis demonstrates excellent degradation performance, which can be attributed to the internal electric field generated by the piezoelectric effect, facilitating the separation of photogenerated electrons and holes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible mechanism of the photo-piezoelectric co-catalysis for CQDs/BCT
ce:section.ce:para:
The catalytic mechanism of CQDs/BCT is proposed, as depicted in Fig. 10 , according to the above results and analysis. Fig. 10 (a) is the schematic diagram of photocatalysis for CQDs/BCT under simulated sunlight. BCT can be only excited by the ultraviolet (UV) of the simulated sunlight to produce photogenerated electrons and holes. The visible and near-infrared light of simulated sunlight can be converted into ultraviolet light through the up-conversion effect of CQDs, which can be then absorbed by BCT to produce photogenerated carriers. A part of the generated holes on the surface can participate in the degradation of RhB, while the other react with OH− in the solution to form ·OH radicals which can also degrade RhB. The generated electrons can transfer to the surface of CQDs and react with dissolved oxygen to form superoxide anions (·O2 −), which also participate in the degradation of RhB. Fig. 10 (b) shows the process of piezoelectric catalysis for CQDs/BCT. Under mechanical vibration, the crystal structure of piezoelectric materials for CQDs/BCT will deform, leading to the separation of positive and negative charge centers, and create an piezoelectric potential across the material [37,38]. Subsequently, the generated electrons and holes by piezoelectric effect will react at surface to form active species and degrade RhB. Fig. 10 (c) shows the process of photo-piezoelectric co-catalysis for CQDs/BCT. This process can be explained by Eqs (4)–(7) [39]. With the up-conversion effect of CQDs, CQDs/BCT can absorb more simulated sunlight to generate active species. Simultaneously, the piezoelectric effect of CQDs/BCT by ultrasonic vibrations will further promote the separation of generated electrons and holes, which can efficiently enhance the redox reactions [40,41].
(4)
C Q D s / B C T
→
u l t r a s o n i c
v i b r a t i o n
s o l a r
r a d i a t i o n
C Q D s / B C T
(
h +
+
e −
)
(5)
h +
+ O
H −
→ ⋅ O H
(6)
e −
+
O 2
→ ⋅
O 2 −
(7)
⋅ O H / ⋅
O 2 −
/
h +
+ D y e
m o l e c u l e → D e c o m p o s i t i o n

In summary, a series of CQDs/BCT composites with excellent catalytic performance were prepared by hydrothermal method. The up-conversion luminescence performance of CQDs and their effect on the catalytic performance of BCT were investigated. Results show that the up-conversion luminescence effect of CQDs can convert visible light and near-infrared light to ultraviolet light which can be absorbed by BCT to enhance the light utilization efficiency. Also, CQDs with high electrical performance can promote the electron transmission and delay electron-hole recombination. As a result, the photocatalytic performance of CQDs/BCT was improved. Especially, 20-CQDs/BCT has obtained the highest degradation rate of RhB of 90.3 % after 120min irradiation. Further, by photo-piezoelectric co-catalysis, 20-CQDs/BCT has obtained an enhanced catalytic performance and completely degraded RhB within 80min. This study can significantly improve the catalytic performance of BCT through the up-conversion luminescence effect of CQDs.

Yunhui Liu: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Chenxiao Zhang: Writing – review & editing. Ting Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Data curation, Conceptualization. Xiaoyang Wang: Writing – review & editing, Validation. Xiaotong Yang: Writing – review & editing, Validation. Dongmei Zeng: Writing – review & editing. Minmin Zou: Writing – review & editing. You Zhang: Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the 2024 Undergraduates Research Training Program.
